subject: Physics
solution:
For three identical conducting bubbles with charge $q$ and radius $r$,
Initial potential on each: $V_i = \frac{kq}{r}$
When three bubbles coalesce,
Total charge $Q = 3q$ and new radius from volume: $\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = 3 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$
So $R = 3^{1/3}r$
Final potential: $V_f = \frac{k(3q)}{3^{1/3}r} = \frac{3kq}{3^{1/3}r} = V_i \cdot 3^{2/3}$
Ratio: $V_i : V_f = 1 : 3^{2/3}$

The work done is moving a charge along an equipotential surface is always zero.
The direction of electric field is perpendicular to the equipotential surface or lines.

subject: Physics
solution:
Potential for uniformly charged solid sphere

v = 1 4 π ε 0 Q r       outside i.e r > R

v = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R   on the surface

v = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 r 2 R 2   inside i.e. r < R

Clearly

   3 v 0 2 , 5 v 0 4  are inside potentials ∵ > v 0

3 v 0 4 , v 0 4  are outside potentials ∵ < v 0

To get R 1 : 3 v 0 2 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 1 2 R 2

where, v 0 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R

3 2 × 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 1 2 R 2

3 2 = 3 2 - 1 2 R 1 2 R 2 ⇒ R 1 = 0

To get R 2 : 5 4 v 0 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 2 2 R 2

5 4 1 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 2 2 R 2

5 4 = 3 2 - 1 2 R 2 2 R 2

1 2 R 2 2 R 2 = 1 4  ⇒ R 2 = R 2

To get R 3 : 3 v 0 4 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3

3 4 1 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3

3 4 R = 1 R 3

R 3 = 4 3 R

To get R 4 : v 0 4 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 4

1 4 × 1 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 4

R 4 = 4 R

R 4 - R 3 = 4 R - 4 R 3 = 8 R 3 > R 2

R 1 = 0  and R 2 < R 4 - R 3

subject: Physics
solution:
Electric field $\mathrm{E}$ at a distance $\mathrm{r}$ due to infinite long wire is $E=\frac{2 k \lambda}{r}$ Force of electron $\Rightarrow F=e E$ $\begin{aligned} & F=e\left(\frac{2 k \lambda}{r}\right) \\ & F=\frac{2 k \lambda e}{r} \end{aligned}$
This force will provide required centripetal force $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{F} & =\frac{\mathrm{m}^2}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{r}} \\ \mathrm{v} & =\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}}} \\ \mathrm{KE} & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}}\right) \\ & =\mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Inside uniformly charged spherical shell, $E=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{III}$

For uniformly charged infinite plate
$E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}$
$\therefore \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{II}$

Outside of spherical shell
$E=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_2}=\frac{\sigma R^2}{\varepsilon_0 r^2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{IV}$

Between two plates $E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}$
$\therefore \mathrm{D} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let's consider the following diagram:

The formula to calculate the electric field due to an infinitely long charged sheet is given by

Es=σ2ε0   ...1

The formula to calculate the electric field due to an infinitely long line charge is given by

El=λ2πε0r   ...2

Using equations (1) and (2), the electric field at P can be calculated as follows:

EP=σ2ε0-14πε02λ3π=σ2ε0-λ6ε0=σ6ε0   ...3

Similarly, the electric field at Q is given by

EQ=σ2ε0-14πε02λ4π=σ2ε0-λ8ε0=σ4ε0   ...4

Divide equation (3) by equation (4) to obtain the required ratio.

EPEQ=σ6ε0σ4ε0=46

Hence, a=6.

subject: Physics
solution:
Charge density, $\rho=\rho_0\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&d q=\rho d v \\
&q_{\text {in }}=\int d q=\rho d v \\
&=\rho_0\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right) 4 \pi r^2 d r \quad\left(\because d v=4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{dr}\right) \\
&=4 \pi \rho_0 \int_0^R\left(1-\frac{r}{R}\right) r^2 d r \\
&=4 \pi \rho_0 \int_0^R r^2 d r-\frac{r^2}{R} d r \\
&=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\left[\frac{r^3}{3}\right]_0^R-\left[\frac{r^4}{4 R}\right]_0^R\right] \\
&=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{R^3}{3}-\frac{R^4}{4 R}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$
$=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{R^3}{3}-\frac{R^3}{4}\right]=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{R^3}{12}\right]$
$q=\frac{\pi \rho_0 R^3}{3}$

$$
E .4 \pi r^2=\left(\frac{\pi \rho_0 R^3}{3 \epsilon_0}\right)
$$
$\therefore \quad$ Electric field outside the ball, $E=\frac{\rho_0 R^3}{12 \in_0 r^2}$

⇒EAEB=105=21

subject: Physics
solution:
Six point charges equally spaced 60° apart on a circle of radius R, with alternating arrangement creating a dipole-like configuration.
Each charge creates an electric field at the center of magnitude $E_0 = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0R^2}$ pointing radially (outward for +Q, inward for -Q).
By vector addition of field contributions from all six charges with proper angle consideration:
The charges at specific positions create a net field with components that depend on their arrangement.
For the given symmetric configuration, the net electric field is: $\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0R^2}(\sqrt{3}\hat{i} - \hat{j})$
This corresponds to magnitude $2E_0$ at angle of $-30°$ from the positive x-axis.
